title: Admin Eye 2-phase broadcast close for clean hand-over
desc: When a new admin Eye spawns, it evicts old admin Eye(s) via 2-phase 'broadcast close' before claiming the wkappbot_elevated pipe. Phase 1 (graceful) signals Global\WKAppBotAdminEyeBroadcastClose named event; listening admins cancel their CTS and exit. Phase 2 (force kill) enumerates wkappbot-core processes and Process.Kill()s them when pipe still present — bootstrap path when old admin lacks the listener. Ensures clean gap (no admin Eye window present) before new takes over, eliminating named-pipe contention.
steps:
WHY THE PATTERN EXISTS
Admin Eye can fall into a state where it accepts pipe connections but hangs on request processing (Bug 1). Without active eviction, simply spawning a new admin Eye fails: the hung one still holds the pipe, new one contends, requests route to the dead one. Plain kill breaks the 'NEVER kill Eye' rule for user Eye — but admin Eye is separate, has no mutex, and can be replaced.

PHASE 1 — GRACEFUL BROADCAST
New admin calls AdminEyeBroadcastClose.FireBroadcast().
Uses Global\WKAppBotAdminEyeBroadcastClose ManualResetEvent.
  ev.Set() → sleep 500ms → ev.Reset()
  poll File.Exists('\.\pipe\wkappbot_elevated') up to 2s
Old admins with StartListener() active observe the signal, cancel their
proxyCts, release pipe, exit process. When no admin holds the pipe,
File.Exists returns false → gap confirmed.

PHASE 2 — FORCE KILL FALLBACK
If pipe still present after the grace period (old admin lacks listener —
typical first-upgrade bootstrap), enumerate wkappbot-core processes and
Process.Kill(entireProcessTree:false) each one except self.
Caller is an already-elevated admin Eye so Kill is authorized.
Guardian respawns user Eye afterward with --sudo marker inherited.

WIRING INTO ADMIN EYE STARTUP
In EyeCommand isElevatedArg branch, immediately after entering the
elevated proxy path:
  AdminEyeBroadcastClose.FireBroadcast();   // before any resource claim
  var proxyCts = new CancellationTokenSource();
  AdminEyeBroadcastClose.StartListener(proxyCts);  // watch for future successors
Then start pipe server and enter main loop — clean slate guaranteed.

SUBPROCESS TIMEOUT BACKSTOP
Inside admin Eye ExecuteCommand, WaitForExitAsync must have a hard
timeout (30s recommended). Without it, a hung subprocess blocks the
request handler forever. With the timeout:
  proc.Kill(entireProcessTree:true) on timeout
  return EyeProxyResponse { ExitCode = -1, Error = 'Subprocess timeout' }
Pipe handler always returns, admin Eye keeps servicing new requests.

CALLER-SIDE FAST-FAIL PAIRING
In Core --sudo proxy-route, ExecuteViaProxy should use a short timeout
(e.g. 1500ms) for the first attempt. If -1 returned, call
LaunchElevatedEye to trigger the spawn → FireBroadcast → fresh admin.
This keeps the user-visible round-trip under ~3s even in worst case.

OBSERVABILITY MARKERS (PULSE + stderr)
Expect to see these during hand-over:
  [EYE:ADMIN-CLOSE] broadcast close fired — waiting for old admin Eye(s) to drain
  [EYE:ADMIN-CLOSE] graceful close did not free pipe — escalating to force kill
  [EYE:ADMIN-CLOSE] force-killed N other wkappbot-core process(es)
  [EYE:ADMIN-CLOSE] pipe cleared after force kill — clean gap
Absence of these while [SUDO] Routing appears means a hand-over never
triggered — hung admin is still live. Investigate subprocess timeouts.

REGRESSION TESTS
bin/wkappbot.hq/experience/tests/sudo/test-mcp-sudo-gate.sh (T1–T10)
  T7 covers admin Eye round-trip; T7d budget 8s verifies fast-fail + recovery path total.
  If T7c/d fail repeatedly across sessions, root cause is inside admin Eye
  ExecuteCommand subprocess spawn — NOT the broadcast-close layer.
author: claude
